summary: Stancil recounts election night from the Harris campaign data team, where extensive technical preparation became irrelevant once the loss was obvious. He argues that the unseen work and contingency plans still mattered as evidence of people fighting on, and that being in the room—win or lose—changes you. The piece ends by urging readers to act, not only to affect outcomes but because participating is less hollow than spectating.
money_quotes: We had plans for every contingency.
The minutes and decimal places didn’t matter.
They were flawlessly delivered disastrous news all night.
Never before have I been involved in trying to answer a more pressing question for a more important audience—and never before has the answer been worse.
It is easier to lose on the field than in the stands.
key_insight: Even when the data work can’t change the outcome, doing it alongside others in the fight gives the effort meaning and changes the people involved.
